access.entityLabel: Wilhelm Heuser, Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle (1907)
access.format: JSON metadata and DOI landing page

localCopies.label: Crossref metadata for Heuser's 1907 article

bibliographic.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, 'Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle,' Anglia 30, new series 18 (1907), 523–548.
bibliographic.title: Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle

entities.description: Heuser's German-language study and first printed edition of both poems. The article describes the roll and its hands, discusses source and metre, states its normalization and expansion practices, and prints the Katherine hymn on pp. 539–547 and the Five Joys hymn on pp. 547–548; printed p. 547 is shared by the end of the first poem and opening of the second.
entities.label: Wilhelm Heuser, Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle (1907)

physicalDescription.extent: printed pp. 523–548 within Anglia volume 30; edited Middle English texts on pp. 539–548
notes: Heuser calls both poems first printings on p. 523. DIMEV gives the article range as pp. 523–548, the Katherine text as pp. 539–547, and visual inspection places the Five Joys text on pp. 547–548 despite a typographical error in DIMEV's subordinate page field.
The article ends with the Five Joys poem, Göttingen dateline, and Heuser signature on printed p. 548; the following scanned page is blank.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1907, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Heuser's 1907 article, edited transcriptions, commentary, apparatus, and typographic presentation

access.accessMethod: Request reference scans through special-collections mediated copying or high-resolution color images through Imaging Services. In the order, request the complete physical roll rather than only the poems: every exposed face and dorse, every membrane, both ends, all joins, tabs or attachments, and rolled and unrolled views, with scale and color target when offered. Identify the work zones separately as the St Katherine hymn from the top through the face and most of the dorse, and the Five Joys poem at the foot of the dorse.
access.format: Reference PDF scans or high-resolution color TIFF images, subject to feasibility, conservation review, quotation, and provider terms
notes: Mediated copying is available to all and currently lists £1 per scan for non-Oxford users. Imaging Services lists a £20 minimum, £27 for the first high-resolution color image and £7.50 for subsequent images from the same item, while large orders are negotiated. Confirm the number of exposures and final quotation before ordering.

The public catalog does not state the number of membranes, joins, or attachments. Ask the repository to enumerate these and confirm that the delivery covers both sides and the complete physical extent.
Ask for the actual order's reuse terms. Research supply or a CC BY-NC grant does not by itself authorize redistribution in a repository connected to commercial or print-on-demand use.

rights.basis: Current Bodleian image terms permit specified personal, noncommercial research, teaching, and qualifying academic uses under CC BY-NC 4.0, but restrict commercial uses and direct publication requests through Imaging Services. The actual order and delivery terms may add item-specific conditions.
rights.component: Any future complete-roll reference or high-resolution images supplied by Bodleian copying or Imaging Services
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and use governed by provider terms
notes: This is prospective guidance, not a rights statement for a file not yet supplied. Preserve and review the actual invoice, delivery notice, and license.
Private or noncommercial research supply does not authorize public repository redistribution, commercial publication, marketing, covers, or for-profit print-on-demand use.

entities.description: The sole manuscript witness of both poems: an unbound parchment roll. The current catalog assigns the St Katherine hymn to a current Anglicana book hand and the later Five Joys poem to a shaky semitextura hand at the foot of the roll. Heuser reports that the first poem occupies the complete face and most of the dorse, with the second added in the remaining space below it on the dorse.

holding.notes: Held by the Bodleian Library, University of Oxford. The current exact catalog record was substantively revised on 2025-03-20.

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Bodl. Rolls 22

physicalDescription.extent: one roll, 65.75 × 11.25 inches; the public catalog does not enumerate its membranes, joins, tabs, attachments, ends, or rolled views
notes: Item 1 is twenty 14-line stanzas, copied in ten written rows per stanza; item 2 is five eight-line stanzas plus a six-line acrostic envoy.
The St Katherine hymn has colored decoration and initials; the Five Joys initials were lightly drafted but not executed, and its hand is later and less formal.

A complete physical audit must include both face and dorse, every membrane and join, upper and lower ends, any tabs or attachments, and rolled and unrolled views. Codex covers, spine, pastedowns, and endleaves are not applicable unless the repository identifies a separate housing as part of the object.

rights.basis: Both works and the manuscript witness date from the beginning of the fifteenth century, many centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms. This does not determine rights or contractual conditions in modern photography, editions, descriptions, or encodings.
rights.component: medieval Middle English textual content of both hymns
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term copyright limits

rights.basis: Current Bodleian image terms permit specified personal, noncommercial research, teaching, and qualifying academic uses under CC BY-NC 4.0, but restrict commercial uses and direct publication requests through Imaging Services. The actual order and delivery terms may add item-specific conditions.

rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and use governed by provider terms

next_steps: Ask mediated copying or Imaging Services for the complete physical roll: every exposed face and dorse, all membranes, both ends, every join, tab or attachment, and rolled and unrolled views, with scale and color target where feasible.
Ask the repository to state the number and order of membranes and whether any housing, detached element, blank area, or attachment belongs to the physical object.
Keep the St Katherine zone and Five Joys zone separately mapped inside the complete delivery, and record the actual order's redistribution, publication, and commercial-use terms.
notes: The negative finding covers the current exact catalog, Digital Bodleian object, and one-canvas IIIF manifest. It is dated and is not proof that no other institutional photography exists.
openQuestions.question: Can Bodleian Libraries supply a complete, auditable digital facsimile of MS. Bodl. Rolls 22 rather than the current single top detail?

next_steps: Consult Ruth Kennedy's full 2003 critical edition and 2004 guild study, compare their authorial and scribal arguments with the complete roll, and retain the repository/DIMEV author attribution as attributed rather than certain meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: What role does the KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG acrostic identify with sufficient confidence: author, commissioner, guild member, or another participant?

next_steps: Review the full Kennedy studies and inspect the complete dorse, drafted initials, hands, and any joins or ownership evidence; preserve the current catalog's competing attributions rather than turning either into a confirmed composition place.
openQuestions.question: Does the PIPWEL acrostic point to Pipewell Abbey in Northamptonshire, Pipwell Manor near Holbeach in south Lincolnshire, a person, or another association?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections for item-level source-selection, keying, and revision records while preserving the directly observed XML-to-print mapping.
openQuestions.question: What item-level U-M production record explains the selection of pp. 539–548 and the 2016 removal of a German introductory fragment?

DIMEV 2983: Katherine the courteous of all that I know
DIMEV 3498: Mildest of mood and meekest of maidens all

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is Heuser's two-poem edited composite, not one homogeneous medieval work and not the complete Anglia article.

The current Bodleian catalog and DIMEV call Richard Spalding the author of the Katherine hymn, but the acrostic does not independently establish whether the name identifies author, patron, or another participant; the attribution remains explicitly qualified.
Physical completeness for this unbound roll is assessed by face, dorse, membranes, ends, joins, attachments, and rolled views, not by codex covers or endleaves.

lineage.summary: CME00071 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan manually keyed and proofread electronic text derived from the text section of Wilhelm Heuser's 1907 Anglia article. The encoding omits Heuser's German manuscript, source, and metrical study on printed pp. 523–538, but retains his editions of two distinct Middle English verse works on pp. 539–548: the twenty-stanza St Katherine hymn attributed through an acrostic to Ricardus Spaldyng, followed by the anonymous five-stanza Hymn on the Five Joys of Mary and its six-line PIPWEL envoy. Both survive in Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. Rolls 22, an unbound parchment roll dated 1400–1410. Heuser reports that the Katherine hymn fills the face and most of the dorse, while the later hand of the Five Joys poem occupies remaining space at the foot of the dorse. A complete 560-page Internet Archive PDF of Anglia volume 30, OCR and structural sidecars are cached. Digital Bodleian currently exposes only one top-of-roll photograph; every canvas in that published selection is cached as partial manuscript coverage, and a mediated whole-roll reproduction route records the need for every exposed face, dorse, membrane, end, join, tab or attachment, and rolled view.

catalogSummary.author: Richard Spaldyng (attributed) and an anonymous poet
catalogSummary.date: Both works are attested in a roll dated 1400–1410; their exact composition dates are unresolved; first printed in 1907
catalogSummary.region: England, with south Lincolnshire authorial and scribal dialect associations; the exact composition places and the meaning of PIPWEL remain unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle

catalogingSubject.label: Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The two-work edited composite represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00071: Wilhelm Heuser's 1907 presentation of the complete Middle English St Katherine hymn attributed through an acrostic to Richard Spaldyng and the distinct anonymous Hymn on the Five Joys of Mary added later to the same parchment roll. This record does not merge their authorship, hands, forms of acrostic, physical zones, or localization histories, and it does not treat Heuser's omitted German article study, the Bodleian roll, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML as the same intellectual object.

contentParts.label: St. Katharina / Alliterative Katherine Hymn

locators.value: top of roll; complete face and most of dorse
one canvas labeled top; stanzas 1–2 and opening of stanza 3 visible

notes: The poem ends on shared printed p. 547 above the start of the Five Joys hymn.
The public image is mapped only to its actual opening overlap and is not treated as a complete facsimile.
contentParts.summary: A twenty-stanza, 280-line Middle English saints' hymn combining Katherine's legend with praise and intercessory prayer; stanzas 17–19 carry the KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG acrostic.

contentParts.label: Hymn on the Five Joys of Mary

locators.value: foot of roll, in remaining space on the dorse, in a later hand

notes: The poem begins on shared printed p. 547 below the close of the Katherine hymn.
The one public Digital Bodleian crop has zero overlap with this poem; no facsimile locator is invented.
The witness's later hand is not treated as evidence for a named author or a separately normalized composition date.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous 46-line Marian hymn in five eight-line stanzas whose initials spell MARIA, followed by a six-line intercessory envoy spelling PIPWEL.

dateStatements.display: Both poems are attested in MS. Bodl. Rolls 22, dated 1400–1410; the Five Joys poem was added in a later hand within the witness's production history
notes: This dates the surviving roll and supplies attestation, not exact composition dates for either poem.
The relative sequence of hands does not justify inventing a distinct date range for the Five Joys composition.

dateStatements.display: Heuser first printed both poems in Anglia volume 30 in 1907
notes: Heuser explicitly describes the two hymns as printed here for the first time.

extent.description: Two top-level XML poem divisions. The St Katherine hymn contains 20 numbered LG elements and exactly 280 L elements; the Five Joys hymn contains five numbered eight-line LG elements plus one unnumbered six-line envoy, totaling 46 L elements. The complete XML has 26 LG, 326 L, 31 NOTE, and 10 PB elements. These are encoding metrics aligned with the institutional and DIMEV stanza descriptions, not a claim about every possible critical lineation.

formStatements.description: Two complete Middle English poems. German headings, footnotes, and the closing dateline are editorial paratext rather than substantive prose parts of the encoded composite.

languageStatements.label: German
notes: German is editorial language around the two Middle English poems; it is not attributed to either medieval poet.

scope.description: Heuser's whole title, part headings, byline, footnotes, and editorial closing context

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: A short heading does not make the medieval poem substantively bilingual.
The preferred whole-record title is Heuser's exact German composite title; the English title is a discovery translation.
The two medieval poems are modeled as independent work parts sharing one roll witness and one Heuser edition, not as one medieval author's oeuvre.
The later Five Joys hand is a physical-witness fact. It is not converted into named authorship, a precise composition date, or a separate physical object.
The public top crop is bound only to the St Katherine part and only to the stanzas actually visible; the Five Joys part has no public image mapping.
German editorial paratext and one Latin subheading are recorded without changing the whole expression's verse form.

next_steps: Review Ruth Kennedy's complete critical edition and guild study and compare their authorial, scribal, and social-context arguments against a complete facsimile; retain the attributed-author label meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: Does the KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG acrostic securely identify the Katherine hymn's author, or could it identify another participant?

next_steps: Review Kennedy's full linguistic and historical analyses and a complete roll facsimile; keep witness production in 1400–1410 separate from composition and preserve Pipewell Abbey and Pipwell Manor as competing associations.
openQuestions.question: What are the defensible composition dates and places of the two poems, and what does PIPWEL identify?

next_steps: Request both face and dorse, every membrane, end, join, tab or attachment, and rolled and unrolled views from Bodleian copying or Imaging Services; map both poem zones separately and obtain explicit reuse terms.
notes: The current public object is only one top-of-roll crop and has no Five Joys overlap.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete whole-roll facsimile be obtained and used for exact physical and typographic research?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections for source-selection and revision documentation while preserving the directly observed XML-to-print mapping.
openQuestions.question: What item-level U-M production record explains omission of pp. 523–538 and removal of an introductory German fragment in 2016?

notes: The current Bodleian catalog summarizes Ruth Kennedy's argument for south Lincolnshire authorial and scribal relicts. This is a dialect association, not a confirmed place of composition for both poems.

scope.description: Modern scholarly analysis of authorial and scribal linguistic relicts

notes: The current catalog reports Kennedy's proposal that PIPWEL refers to Pipwell Manor near Holbeach; the acrostic's exact referent is unresolved.

scope.description: Possible referent of the PIPWEL acrostic

notes: Heuser and MLGB3 connect PIPWEL with the Cistercian abbey at Pipewell, Northamptonshire, but the current catalog preserves Kennedy's competing Lincolnshire interpretation. Neither is cataloged as a confirmed composition place.

scope.description: Older possible provenance inferred from the PIPWEL acrostic

notes: Göttingen is Heuser's editorial place, not a medieval place of composition or copying.

scope.description: Heuser's editorial dateline at the end of the printed composite

notes: Stanzas 17–19 form the acrostic KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG. Heuser, the current Bodleian catalog, and DIMEV interpret the name as authorial, but the acrostic alone does not eliminate other historical roles.
Heuser separately argues that the surviving copy is not the original and that its calligraphic scribe is unlikely to be the composer; author and witness scribe are not conflated.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous poet of the Five Joys hymn
notes: The MARIA and PIPWEL acrostics do not name a person. The later hand is a witness-level fact and is not treated as proof of a distinct author-scribe identity.
Heuser prints both poems directly from MS. Bodl. Rolls 22 and states his normalization and abbreviation-expansion practices.

scope.description: Footnotes, supplied readings, and immediate editorial headings retained in CME00071

subjects.label: Virgin martyrdom, chastity, persecution, and conversion
Five Joys of the Virgin Mary
notes: The five stanzas praise the Annunciation, Nativity, Resurrection and Ascension, Assumption, and Coronation, followed by a six-line prayer.

summaries.value: The first poem is a twenty-stanza alliterative hymn to Saint Katherine of Alexandria. It recounts her disputation with fifty rhetoricians, their conversion and martyrdom, the conversions and deaths of the empress and Porphyry, Katherine's torture and beheading, the translation of her body to Sinai, and her power to intercede, before closing in direct prayer. Stanzas 17–19 form the acrostic KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG. The second and formally independent poem praises five joys of Mary—the Annunciation, Christ's Nativity, Resurrection and Ascension, Mary's Assumption, and Coronation—and ends by asking her intercession. Its stanza initials spell MARIA and the six-line envoy spells PIPWEL.

CME00071 begins on Heuser's printed p. 539 with the St Katherine text, crosses shared p. 547 from the end of that poem into the Five Joys hymn, and ends with Heuser's signature on p. 548. It omits the German article title and study on pp. 523–538. In the physical roll, the first poem occupies the face and most of the dorse; the second is a later-hand addition at the foot of the dorse. The sole public image is only a cropped top detail of the opening St Katherine stanzas.

titles.value: Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle

notes: Discovery translation of Heuser's German title; it is not presented as a historical title.

titles.value: Katereyn, þe curteys of alle þat I know
Hymne auf die fünf Freuden der Maria
Poem on the Five Joys of the Virgin

notes: The XML prints the subheading 'Scā Maria'; DIMEV records Sancta Maria as an attributed title at the start.

work.summary: The first poem is a twenty-stanza alliterative hymn to Saint Katherine of Alexandria. It recounts her disputation with fifty rhetoricians, their conversion and martyrdom, the conversions and deaths of the empress and Porphyry, Katherine's torture and beheading, the translation of her body to Sinai, and her power to intercede, before closing in direct prayer. Stanzas 17–19 form the acrostic KATERINA RICARDUS SPALDYNG. The second and formally independent poem praises five joys of Mary—the Annunciation, Christ's Nativity, Resurrection and Ascension, Mary's Assumption, and Coronation—and ends by asking her intercession. Its stanza initials spell MARIA and the six-line envoy spells PIPWEL.
